panel.pairing: Ciao, come stai? — Hi, how are you?
panel.mixology: Informal, for both hello and goodbye. The 'c' before 'i' is a 'ch' sound.
panel.examples: World-famous and casual. Use only with friends and peers; for formality use 'salve' or 'buongiorno'. Originally from Venetian 'your slave (servant)'.

panel.pairing: Buongiorno, signora. — Good morning, ma'am.
panel.mixology: 'Buon' (good) + 'giorno' (day). The 'gi' is a soft 'j' sound.
panel.examples: The standard polite daytime greeting. Switches to 'buonasera' in the late afternoon.

panel.pairing: Buonasera a tutti. — Good evening, everyone.
panel.mixology: 'Buona' (good, fem.) + 'sera' (evening). Used as both greeting and farewell in the evening.
panel.examples: Begin using it from the late afternoon onward. For bedtime, switch to 'buonanotte'.

panel.pairing: Buonanotte, a domani. — Good night, see you tomorrow.
panel.mixology: 'Buona' + 'notte' (night). Double 't' is held slightly longer — a feature of Italian.
panel.examples: Only for going to sleep, like English 'good night'. Don't use it as an evening greeting.

panel.pairing: Arrivederci, grazie. — Goodbye, thank you.
panel.mixology: Literally 'to seeing each other again'. The polite, neutral goodbye. Formal version: 'arrivederLa'.
panel.examples: More polite than 'ciao' for goodbye. The rolled 'r' and the 'ci' (chee) sound are good pronunciation practice.

panel.pairing: A presto! — See you soon!
panel.mixology: 'A' (to/until) + 'presto' (soon/early). A warm farewell.
panel.examples: Use when you'll see someone again shortly. 'A dopo' (see you later) is another common option.

panel.pairing: Come stai? — Bene! — How are you? — Good!
panel.mixology: Uses 'stare' (to be/stay) for wellbeing, informal 'tu'. Formal version: 'Come sta?'.
panel.examples: Italian uses 'stare' (not 'essere') to ask how someone is. The formal 'Come sta?' is for strangers and elders.

panel.pairing: Sto bene, grazie. — I'm well, thanks.
panel.mixology: An adverb — invariable. 'Molto bene' = very well; 'benissimo' = great.
panel.examples: The standard reply to 'come stai?'. Stack it up: 'bene' → 'molto bene' → 'benissimo'.

panel.pairing: Piacere, sono Marco. — Nice to meet you, I'm Marco.
panel.mixology: Literally 'pleasure'. Also the verb 'to please/like'. As a greeting it's a fixed phrase.
panel.examples: Said when meeting someone. A common fuller form is 'piacere di conoscerti' (pleasure to meet you).

panel.pairing: Come ti chiami? — Mi chiamo Anna. — What's your name? — I'm Anna.
panel.mixology: Reflexive 'chiamarsi' (to call oneself), informal. The 'chi' is a hard 'k' sound: 'kyah-mee'.
panel.examples: Answer 'Mi chiamo…' (I'm called…). Formal version: 'Come si chiama?'.

panel.pairing: Sì, grazie. — Yes, thank you.
panel.mixology: Accent matters: 'sì' (yes) vs 'si' (the reflexive 'oneself'). Pronounced 'see'.
panel.examples: The accent on 'sì' distinguishes 'yes' from the unstressed 'si'. A small mark, a big difference.

panel.pairing: No, grazie. — No, thank you.
panel.mixology: Invariable. To negate a verb, place 'non' before it ('non parlo' = I don't speak).
panel.examples: Note: 'no' is the answer 'no', but 'non' is the word that negates verbs. Don't mix them up.

panel.pairing: Un caffè, per favore. — A coffee, please.
panel.mixology: Literally 'for favor'. A fixed phrase; 'per piacere' and 'per cortesia' are alternatives.
panel.examples: The standard 'please'. Italians have several polite variants; 'per favore' is the most common and safe.

panel.pairing: Grazie mille. — Thanks a lot.
panel.mixology: Invariable. The 'z' is a 'ts' sound. 'Grazie mille' (a thousand thanks) intensifies it.
panel.examples: The 'zie' ending sounds like 'tsyeh'. 'Grazie mille' is the everyday way to say 'thanks so much'.

panel.pairing: — Grazie. — Prego. — Thanks. — You're welcome.
panel.mixology: From 'pregare' (to pray/beg). A versatile word: 'you're welcome', 'please go ahead', 'after you'.
panel.examples: Wonderfully multipurpose: it answers 'grazie', invites someone to go first, or means 'please, help yourself'.

panel.pairing: Scusi, dov'è la stazione? — Excuse me, where's the station?
panel.mixology: Formal command from 'scusare'. Informal is 'scusa'.
panel.examples: Use 'scusi' (formal) with strangers, 'scusa' (informal) with friends, to apologize or get attention.

panel.pairing: Mi dispiace molto. — I'm very sorry.
panel.mixology: Literally 'it displeases me' — the thing is the subject, like 'mi piace'. For genuine regret.
panel.examples: Deeper than 'scusa': use 'mi dispiace' for real apology or sympathy, 'scusa' for minor bumps.

panel.pairing: Scusi, non capisco. — Sorry, I don't understand.
panel.mixology: From 'capire' (to understand), an '-isc-' verb. 'Non' negates it.
panel.examples: A survival phrase. Follow with 'può ripetere?' (can you repeat?) to keep things moving.

panel.pairing: Parla inglese? — Do you speak English?
panel.mixology: Formal 'Lei' form of 'parlare'. Informal: 'Parli inglese?'. 'Inglese' is lowercase.
panel.examples: Italian doesn't capitalize languages. The formal 'parla' vs informal 'parli' is a one-letter politeness shift.

panel.pairing: Uno, due, tre. — One, two, three.
panel.mixology: Becomes 'un'/'uno' (masc.) or 'una'/'un'' (fem.) as the article 'a/an', depending on the next sound.
panel.examples: Like 'a/an', 'uno' shifts form to match the noun: 'un caffè', 'una pizza', 'un'amica'.

panel.pairing: Due caffè, per favore. — Two coffees, please.
panel.mixology: Invariable. Two clear vowels: 'DOO-eh'.
panel.examples: From two on, Italian numbers don't change for gender. Every vowel is pronounced distinctly.

panel.pairing: Sono le tre. — It's three o'clock.
panel.mixology: Invariable. Just one syllable: 'treh'.
panel.examples: Used for counting and telling time: 'sono le tre' = it's three o'clock.

panel.pairing: Ho dieci euro. — I have ten euros.
panel.mixology: Invariable. The 'ci' is a 'chee' sound: 'DYEH-chee'.
panel.examples: Note 'euro' stays singular in Italian even when counting: 'dieci euro', not 'euros'.

panel.pairing: Cento euro. — A hundred euros.
panel.mixology: Invariable, even in multiples ('duecento' = 200, written as one word).
panel.examples: Unlike Spanish or French, 'cento' never changes and joins to its multiplier: 'duecento', 'trecento'.

panel.pairing: Che ora è? — Sono le due. — What time is it? — It's two.
panel.mixology: Literally 'what hour is it?'. Answer 'è l'una' (1:00) or 'sono le…' (other hours).
panel.examples: Singular 'è l'una' for one o'clock, plural 'sono le…' for the rest — the same pattern as Spanish.

panel.pairing: Oggi è lunedì. — Today is Monday.
panel.mixology: Adverb of time. Double 'g' before 'i' gives a soft 'j': 'OH-jee'.
panel.examples: Pairs with 'ieri' (yesterday) and 'domani' (tomorrow) — the essential time trio.

panel.pairing: A domani! — See you tomorrow!
panel.mixology: Adverb of time. 'A domani' is a common goodbye. 'Dopodomani' = the day after tomorrow.
panel.examples: Unlike Spanish 'mañana', Italian 'domani' means only 'tomorrow'; morning is 'mattina'.

panel.pairing: Andiamo adesso. — Let's go now.
panel.mixology: Adverb. 'Ora' is a common synonym ('now', also 'hour').
panel.examples: Both 'adesso' and 'ora' mean 'now'. 'Subito' means 'immediately / right away'.

panel.pairing: Un bicchiere d'acqua, per favore. — A glass of water, please.
panel.mixology: Feminine: 'l'acqua'. The 'cqu' is a held 'kw' sound, unique to a few Italian words.
panel.examples: Ask for 'acqua naturale' (still) or 'acqua frizzante' (sparkling) — Italians will often ask which you want.

panel.pairing: Un caffè, per favore. — A coffee, please.
panel.mixology: Masculine: 'il caffè'. The accent on the final 'è' means the stress lands there: 'kahf-FEH'.
panel.examples: Ordering 'un caffè' gets you an espresso. A 'caffè latte' is coffee with milk — but just 'latte' will get you a glass of milk!

panel.pairing: Del pane, per favore. — Some bread, please.
panel.mixology: Masculine: 'il pane'. Two clear syllables: 'PAH-neh'.
panel.examples: A staple at every Italian meal, usually served without butter. A bakery is 'il forno' or 'la panetteria'.

panel.pairing: Il conto, per favore. — The check, please.
panel.mixology: Masculine: 'il conto'. From 'contare' (to count).
panel.examples: As elsewhere in Europe, the bill comes only when you ask. Catch the waiter with 'il conto, per favore'.

panel.pairing: Un bicchiere di vino rosso. — A glass of red wine.
panel.mixology: Masculine: 'il vino'. 'Vino rosso' (red), 'vino bianco' (white).
panel.examples: Central to Italian dining. 'Un quartino' is a quarter-litre carafe — a handy, common house-wine order.

panel.pairing: Il cibo è ottimo. — The food is excellent.
panel.mixology: Masculine: 'il cibo'. The 'ci' is 'chee': 'CHEE-boh'. A general word for food.
panel.examples: 'Il cibo' is food in general. For a specific dish you'd say 'il piatto'; a meal is 'il pasto'.

panel.pairing: Vorrei un caffè. — I'd like a coffee.
panel.mixology: Polite conditional of 'volere' (to want). Softer than 'voglio' (I want).
panel.examples: The courteous way to order. 'Vorrei…' (I'd like) is gentler than the blunt 'voglio…' (I want).

panel.pairing: Il menù, per favore. — The menu, please.
panel.mixology: Masculine: 'il menù', with the stress on the final 'u'. A loanword from French.
panel.examples: Unlike French/Spanish, in Italy 'il menù' simply means the menu of dishes. 'Il menù fisso' is a set meal.

panel.pairing: Salute! — Cheers!
panel.mixology: Literally 'health'. Used as a toast and after a sneeze. 'Cin cin' is the casual clink-glasses toast.
panel.examples: Toast with 'salute' or the playful 'cin cin'. Like Spanish 'salud', it also means 'bless you' after a sneeze.

panel.pairing: Dov'è la stazione? — Where is the station?
panel.mixology: 'Dove' (where) + 'è' (is) contracts to 'dov'è'. Plural: 'dove sono…?'.
panel.examples: The essential travel question. 'Dove' elides with 'è' to 'dov'è' — a smooth, common contraction.

panel.pairing: Dov'è il bagno? — Where's the bathroom?
panel.mixology: Masculine: 'il bagno'. The 'gn' is a 'ny' sound, like Spanish 'ñ'.
panel.examples: 'Il bagno' covers both bathroom and bath. In a bar or restaurant you can also ask for 'la toilette'.

panel.pairing: Giri a sinistra. — Turn left.
panel.mixology: Feminine, used in 'a sinistra' (to the left).
panel.examples: Direction basics: 'a sinistra' (left), 'a destra' (right), 'dritto' / 'sempre dritto' (straight ahead).

panel.pairing: È a destra. — It's on the right.
panel.mixology: Feminine: 'la destra'. Don't confuse with 'dritto' (straight ahead).
panel.examples: Trap to watch: 'a destra' = to the right, but 'dritto' = straight ahead. Listen carefully when getting directions.

panel.pairing: Sono qui. — I'm here.
panel.mixology: Adverb of place. 'Qui' and 'qua' both mean 'here', used almost interchangeably.
panel.examples: Italian has two near-identical 'here' words (qui/qua) and two for 'there' (lì/là). All are common.

panel.pairing: È là. — It's over there.
panel.mixology: Adverb of place, with accent: 'là' (there) vs 'la' (the). 'Lì' is a synonym.
panel.examples: The accent distinguishes 'là' (there) from 'la' (the feminine article). 'Lì' and 'là' both mean 'there'.

panel.pairing: La stazione è vicina. — The station is nearby.
panel.mixology: Feminine: 'la stazione'. Words ending in '-zione' are feminine (like Spanish '-ción').
panel.examples: A reliable rule: '-zione' nouns are feminine ('la stazione', 'la lezione'). The 'zi' sounds like 'tsy'.

panel.pairing: Quanto costa? — How much is it?
panel.mixology: From 'costare' (to cost). For multiple items: 'quanto costano?'.
panel.examples: The key shopping question. 'Quant'è?' (how much is it?) is a shorter, common alternative.

panel.pairing: Io sono italiano. — I am Italian.
panel.mixology: Subject pronoun. Often dropped, since the verb ending shows the subject ('sono' = I am).
panel.examples: Like Spanish, Italian usually omits subject pronouns — 'sono' alone means 'I am'. Use 'io' for emphasis.

panel.pairing: Tu parli italiano? — Do you speak Italian?
panel.mixology: Informal singular 'you'. The formal equivalent is 'Lei' (capitalized).
panel.examples: Use 'tu' with friends and peers; switch to the formal 'Lei' for strangers, elders, and business.

panel.pairing: Lui è alto. Lei è alta. — He is tall. She is tall.
panel.mixology: 'Lui' (he), 'lei' (she). Capitalized 'Lei' is also the formal 'you'. Adjectives agree: 'alto'/'alta'.
panel.examples: 'Lei' does double duty: lowercase 'lei' = she, capitalized 'Lei' = formal 'you'. Adjective endings shift by gender.

panel.pairing: Noi siamo amici. — We are friends.
panel.mixology: Subject pronoun 'we'. Two vowels: 'NOH-ee'.
panel.examples: The verb ending ('siamo' = we are) already signals 'we', so 'noi' is often dropped except for emphasis.

panel.pairing: È il mio migliore amico. — He's my best friend.
panel.mixology: 'l'amico' (male), 'l'amica' (female). Plurals: 'amici' (the 'c' softens to 'chee') / 'amiche' (stays hard 'k').
panel.examples: A spelling quirk: masculine plural 'amici' is 'ah-MEE-chee', but feminine 'amiche' keeps the hard 'k' sound.

panel.pairing: La mia famiglia è grande. — My family is big.
panel.mixology: Feminine: 'la famiglia'. The 'gli' is a liquid 'ly' sound, distinctive to Italian.
panel.examples: The 'gli' sound (as in 'famiglia', 'figlio') has no English equivalent — somewhere between 'ly' and 'lli'.

panel.pairing: Mia madre si chiama Sofia. — My mother's name is Sofia.
panel.mixology: Feminine: 'la madre'. Affectionate: 'mamma'.
panel.examples: Note: with singular family members you drop the article — 'mia madre', not 'la mia madre'. 'Mamma' is the warm term.

panel.pairing: Mio padre lavora molto. — My father works a lot.
panel.mixology: Masculine: 'il padre'. 'I genitori' means 'the parents'. Affectionate: 'papà'.
panel.examples: Italian uses a separate word, 'i genitori', for 'parents' (not the plural of padre). Affectionate: 'papà' (stress on the end).

panel.pairing: L'uomo e la donna. — The man and the woman.
panel.mixology: 'l'uomo' (man, irregular plural 'uomini'), 'la donna' (woman, plural 'donne').
panel.examples: 'Uomo' has an irregular plural: one 'uomo', many 'uomini'. 'Donna' is regular: 'donne'.

panel.pairing: Sono stanco. — I am tired.
panel.mixology: Highly irregular: sono, sei, è, siamo, siete, sono. Used for identity, origin, traits.
panel.examples: Italian, like Spanish, has two 'to be' verbs. 'Essere' is for permanent identity and is the main past-tense helper.

panel.pairing: Ho due fratelli. — I have two brothers.
panel.mixology: Irregular: ho, hai, ha, abbiamo, avete, hanno. The 'h' is always silent.
panel.examples: Used for age and hunger like Spanish: 'ho fame' (I'm hungry), 'ho 20 anni' (I'm 20). The silent 'h' distinguishes 'ho' from 'o' (or).

panel.pairing: Come stai? — Sto bene. — How are you? — I'm well.
panel.mixology: Irregular: sto, stai, sta, stiamo, state, stanno. Used for health, location, and ongoing actions.
panel.examples: Italian uses 'stare' (not 'essere') for how you feel and for the '-ing' present: 'sto mangiando' = I'm eating.

panel.pairing: Vado a casa. — I'm going home.
panel.mixology: Irregular: vado, vai, va, andiamo, andate, vanno.
panel.examples: 'Andiamo!' (let's go!) is one of the most useful exclamations you'll hear and use in Italy.

panel.pairing: Parlo un po' di italiano. — I speak a little Italian.
panel.mixology: Regular '-are' verb — the model for the largest group. parlo, parli, parla, parliamo, parlate, parlano.
panel.examples: Learn 'parlare' and you have the pattern for thousands of '-are' verbs, Italian's biggest verb family.

panel.pairing: Vuoi mangiare qualcosa? — Do you want to eat something?
panel.mixology: '-are' verb with a spelling tweak: the 'i' disappears in 'mangi', 'mangiamo' to keep the soft 'g'.
panel.examples: A near-regular '-are' verb. The silent 'i' in 'mangiamo' just preserves the soft 'g' (j) sound.

panel.pairing: Voglio un caffè. — I want a coffee.
panel.mixology: Irregular: voglio, vuoi, vuole, vogliamo, volete, vogliono. The 'gli' is that liquid 'ly' sound.
panel.examples: 'Voglio' (I want) is direct; 'vorrei' (I'd like) is the polite form for ordering and requests.

panel.pairing: Puoi aiutarmi? — Can you help me?
panel.mixology: Irregular: posso, puoi, può, possiamo, potete, possono. Followed by an infinitive.
panel.examples: Essential for requests: 'può…?' (formal) / 'puoi…?' (informal) = 'can you…?'. 'Posso?' = 'may I?'.

panel.pairing: Che cosa fai? — What are you doing?
panel.mixology: Irregular: faccio, fai, fa, facciamo, fate, fanno. Covers both 'do' and 'make'.
panel.examples: Also drives weather and many idioms: 'fa caldo' (it's hot), 'fare una foto' (to take a photo). A workhorse verb.

panel.pairing: — Grazie. — Nessun problema. — Thanks. — No problem.
panel.mixology: 'Nessun' (no/none) + 'problema'. Note 'problema' is masculine despite ending in '-a'.
panel.examples: A relaxed reply to thanks. Watch out: 'il problema' is masculine — a common exception to the '-a = feminine' rule.

panel.pairing: — Andiamo? — D'accordo. — Shall we go? — OK.
panel.mixology: From 'essere d'accordo' (to agree). 'D'accordo' alone = OK / agreed.
panel.examples: The everyday 'OK'. 'Sono d'accordo' = 'I agree'. Very common in conversation.

panel.pairing: — Vieni? — Certo! — Are you coming? — Of course!
panel.mixology: An adverb/adjective. 'Certo!' on its own = of course! 'Certamente' is the fuller form.
panel.examples: An enthusiastic 'of course!'. The 'ce' sounds like 'cheh': 'CHEHR-toh'.

panel.pairing: Ho fame, mangiamo? — I'm hungry, shall we eat?
panel.mixology: Uses 'avere' (to have) + 'fame' (hunger): literally 'I have hunger'. The 'h' is silent.
panel.examples: Like Spanish and French, Italian 'has' hunger. Similarly 'ho sete' = I'm thirsty.

panel.pairing: Scusi, mi sono perso. — Sorry, I'm lost.
panel.mixology: Reflexive past of 'perdersi' (to get lost). A woman says 'persa'.
panel.examples: The ending agrees with gender: a man says 'perso', a woman 'persa'. A vital travel phrase.

panel.pairing: Mi piace il caffè. — I like coffee.
panel.mixology: From 'piacere' — works backwards: literally 'it is pleasing to me'. Plural things: 'mi piacciono'.
panel.examples: Like Spanish 'me gusta', the thing liked is the subject: 'mi piacciono i gatti' = 'cats are pleasing to me' = I like cats.

panel.pairing: Buon appetito! — Enjoy your meal!
panel.mixology: Literally 'good appetite'. A fixed phrase, said before eating.
panel.examples: Always said before a meal in Italy. The polite reply, if someone says it to you, is 'grazie, altrettanto' (thanks, you too).

panel.pairing: Quant'è? — How much is it?
panel.mixology: 'Quanto' (how much) + 'è' (is), contracted to 'quant'è'. The casual price question.
panel.examples: 'Quant'è?' is the quick spoken form; 'quanto costa?' is slightly more complete. Both are everywhere.
